name: Tadalafil changed exercise stress-axis hormones without improving performance parameters
statement: Two tadalafil 20 mg doses reduced ACTH, cortisol, corticosterone, and free cortisol index responses to maximal exercise and increased beta-endorphin AUC and DHEAS-to-cortisol ratio during recovery, but did not influence cardiorespiratory or performance parameters.
population_or_context: Healthy males undergoing maximal cycle exercise with serial endocrine sampling.
time_horizon: Two doses over 36 hours and immediate exercise recovery.
quantitative_note: Abstract reports lower ACTH, cortisol, corticosterone, and FCI responses and higher beta-endorphin AUC/DHEAS-to-cortisol ratio versus placebo, without performance/cardiorespiratory changes.

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase_note: Abstract reports crossover design, endocrine outcomes, and no performance/cardiorespiratory effect.

caveats: No validated recovery or performance benefit.
population_profile: Healthy males; mechanistically relevant but not direct benefit evidence.
tadalafil_exposure: Two tadalafil 20 mg doses with 36-hour interval.
endpoint_measure: ACTH, cortisol, corticosterone, free cortisol index, beta-endorphin, DHEAS-to-cortisol ratio, cardiorespiratory/performance parameters.
effect_estimate: Endocrine stress-axis modulation without cardiorespiratory or performance improvement.
safety_findings: Authors state further studies are needed to identify possible health risks and clinical uses.
directness_to_target_population: Moderate for acute endocrine physiology in healthy males; low for performance, mood, recovery, or health claims.
confounding_and_bias_limits: Small sample, surrogate endocrine endpoints, acute exposure, and no clinical performance benefit.
class_transfer_limits: Direct tadalafil evidence, but endocrine changes should not be transferred into ergogenic claims.
